Design considerations: temporary checkout of file contents, isolated .shatter state, clear UX for what 'analysis results' mean when not analyzing current code. Deferred until the basic --changed/--since feature is proven.
title: Time-travel analysis: --since with end boundary before HEAD

description:
All Go PBT is roundtrip serialization. No behavioral properties on:

1. Analysis: arbitrary Go source snippets (valid function signatures) → analyzer extracts correct parameter count and types. Use rapid to generate simple Go function signatures.
2. Instrumentation: instrumented code preserves original function behavior — for simple functions, executing instrumented vs original with same inputs produces same output.
3. Mock generation: sanitizeMockName is idempotent (sanitize(sanitize(x)) == sanitize(x)) and always produces valid Go identifiers (starts with letter/underscore, contains only alphanumeric/underscore).
4. Executor: for any valid function + valid inputs, executor either returns a result or a structured error (never panics, never hangs past timeout).
5. Protocol handler: for any sequence of valid protocol messages, handler produces valid responses in the same order (no reordering, no drops).
title: Go semantic property tests beyond roundtrips

close_reason: ConstValue::Complex added to sym_expr.rs with kind+repr design. ConcreteValue::Complex added to solver.rs. Solver unwraps Complex repr for Z3 solving. concrete_to_json handles Complex. All 406 tests pass, clippy clean.
description:
Extend the symbolic expression and constraint solver to handle complex type constants.

## What
1. Add ConstValue::Complex { kind: ComplexKind, repr: Box<ConstValue> } to shatter-core/src/sym_expr.rs
2. Add ConcreteValue::Complex { kind: ComplexKind, repr: Box<ConcreteValue> } to shatter-core/src/solver.rs
3. Handle ConstValue::Complex in the solver's to_z3_expr by unwrapping repr and recursing

## Why
When a frontend instruments code like `if (date > new Date("2024-01-01"))`, it needs to emit a constant that preserves the complex type identity while carrying an underlying primitive the solver can reason about. For dates, the repr is Int(epoch_millis); for bigint, Str(digit_string); for char, Int(codepoint). The solver unwraps repr and reasons about the primitive directly — no Z3 changes needed for Tier 1 solvable types.

## Design
- ConstValue::Complex { kind, repr } where repr is the underlying value Z3 reasons about
- ConcreteValue::Complex { kind, repr } for solved values that need to be tagged in the wire format
- Solver handles Complex by recursing: ConstValue::Complex { repr, .. } => to_z3_const(repr, hint_sort)

## Files
- shatter-core/src/sym_expr.rs — ConstValue::Complex variant
- shatter-core/src/solver.rs — ConcreteValue::Complex variant + to_z3_expr handling

## Tests
- Round-trip serialization for ConstValue::Complex with various repr types (Int, Str, Float)
- Round-trip serialization for ConcreteValue::Complex
- Solver correctly unwraps Complex(Date, Int(epoch)) in comparisons
- Solver returns Complex(Date, Int(solved_epoch)) in results
title: Core: ConstValue::Complex, ConcreteValue::Complex, and solver unwrap

close_reason: Implemented Shannon entropy analysis for crypto detection with utilities in all frontends
description:
Layer 3 of crypto function recognition (str-mxa3). At runtime, measure Shannon entropy of input and output buffers for suspected functions to confirm crypto behavior.

Detection signal: decryption transforms high-entropy input (ciphertext, ~8 bits/byte) into lower-entropy output (plaintext, typically 4-6 bits/byte for text, variable for structured data). Encryption is the inverse.

Implementation:
- Entropy measurement utility in each frontend (simple byte-frequency histogram → Shannon formula)
- Hook candidate functions (from Layer 1 registry or Layer 2 naming) at runtime
- Compare input vs output entropy; flag significant delta (threshold TBD, likely >1.5 bits/byte difference)
- Report confirmed crypto boundaries to core engine with measured entropy values

Edge cases:
- Compressed plaintext has high entropy (close to ciphertext) — may need secondary signals
- Small buffers (<32 bytes) have unreliable entropy estimates — require minimum size threshold
- Authenticated encryption modes (GCM, Poly1305) may fail/throw on tampered input — handle gracefully

Blocked on str-38zx (Layer 1 frontend integration + protocol message) — Layer 3 uses the same protocol message format and hooks into the same frontend analysis pipeline.
title: Runtime entropy analysis for crypto detection

The scan output code that populates file_path needs to relativize paths against the project root.

description: Implement shrink_candidates(value: &Value, type_info: &TypeInfo) -> Vec<Value> — the inverse of mutate_inputs. Given a value, produce progressively simpler variants: Numbers: halve toward zero, try 0, 1, -1. Strings: remove last/first char, try empty, single char. Booleans: try false. Arrays: remove last/first element, try empty. Objects: remove optional fields one at a time. Nullable: try null. Shared by both minimal witness shrinking and boundary refinement. Related to but distinct from str-e0a4's mutation — mutation goes toward novel, shrinking goes toward simpler.
title: Shrink candidates function (shared infrastructure)

close_reason: All features implemented in commit 1560cd5: --output, --clean, --dry-run, stale command, fingerprinting, incremental merge. Only str-w8d (call-graph fingerprints) remains.
description:
## Problem

Shatter's CLI currently bundles discovery, analysis, and exploration into monolithic commands (scan, run) with outputs going to hidden .shatter/cache/ or shatter-report/. This makes it unusable with Make, which needs predictable input→output file relationships with timestamp-based staleness.

The lasting output of Shatter is behavioral specifications — not human reports. Specs are the precise, complete, machine-diffable records of observed behavior. They should be first-class project artifacts, not hidden cache.

## Design

### Two first-class artifacts, per-source-file granularity

1. **Spec files** (FunctionSpec[]) — the lasting, diffable behavioral specification. Versioned in git. Used by spec-diff for regression detection.
2. **Behavior map files** (BehaviorMap[]) — input→output mappings for compositional mocking. Stored separately, may or may not be versioned.

Both are per-source-file: specs/src/math.ts.json contains specs for all functions in math.ts.

### Internal function fingerprinting for performance

When a source file changes, Shatter doesn't blindly re-explore all functions:
1. Run cheap analyze phase → extract function signatures/ASTs
2. Compute per-function fingerprint (hash of source text, parameter types, branch structure)
3. Compare against fingerprints stored in existing output
4. Only re-explore functions with changed fingerprints
5. Merge results: preserve unchanged specs, update changed ones

Fingerprints should compose across the call graph: if B's behavior changes, A (which calls B) needs re-exploration even if A's source is unchanged.

### CLI changes needed

1. **explore --output <path>** — write spec to explicit path (Make target)
2. **explore accepts file-level targets** — shatter explore src/math.ts explores all functions in the file
3. **shatter stale** — lightweight staleness check (exit code 0/1) using fingerprints, for non-Make incremental workflows
4. **explore --clean** — ignore existing specs/fingerprints and force full re-exploration from scratch. Escape hatch for when fingerprints are stale, cache is corrupted, or exploration heuristics have changed.
5. **explore --dry-run** — run analyze + fingerprint comparison, print what would be explored (which functions are stale, which would be skipped), but don't actually run the concolic engine. Useful for understanding what Make would trigger, debugging fingerprint behavior, and CI cost estimation.

### Makefile usage

```makefile
SPECS_DIR := specs

$(SPECS_DIR)/src/math.ts.json: src/math.ts
    shatter explore src/math.ts -o $@

$(SPECS_DIR)/src/utils.ts.json: src/utils.ts
    shatter explore src/utils.ts -o $@
```

### What stays the same

- scan and run remain as batteries-included commands for users not using Make
- .shatter/cache/ remains for internal caching (behavior maps for compositional mocking during exploration)

description:
Run the entire Shatter tool (core + frontends) inside a container or microVM for isolation. The target project directory on the host would be bind-mounted (containers) or virtiofs-mounted (microVMs) into the execution environment, avoiding checkout/build steps.

Motivation:
- Shatter executes arbitrary user code with adversarially-generated inputs — textbook sandboxing use case
- Whole-tool isolation is simpler than per-subprocess: one boundary, one mount, one lifecycle
- Standard UX: docker run -v $(pwd):/work shatter explore /work/src/foo.ts
- Also solves distribution friction (eliminates multi-toolchain install)

Output handling:
- Default: read-write bind mount of project directory — Shatter writes .shatter/ cache and generated tests directly, visible on host immediately
- Optional split mount for defense-in-depth: source read-only, output paths (.shatter/, generated-tests/) read-write
- Container must not require users to pre-create output directories — entrypoint should mkdir -p as needed

Implementation considerations:
- Start with OCI container (Dockerfile) — ubiquitous tooling, bind mounts are native speed
- MicroVMs (Firecracker, Cloud Hypervisor) worth considering if Shatter is ever offered as a hosted service
- Container image needs: Rust runtime, Node.js 22+, Go 1.24+, Z3 libs
- Mount target project read-only where possible; output directory read-write
- Preserve stdio streaming for progress output
title: Containerized whole-tool execution

description:
Current examples are purpose-built test fixtures. Add more realistic utility functions that exercise complex branching naturally, making the demo more compelling and testing the engine against real-world patterns.

**User-proposed candidates:**
- Roman numeral converter (int→roman and roman→int) — lots of conditional mapping, validation of malformed input
- URL parser — protocol detection, authority/path/query/fragment splitting, edge cases (relative URLs, punycode, missing scheme)
- Email address validator — local-part rules, domain validation, quoted strings, plus-addressing, length limits

**Additional candidates to consider:**
- Semver parser/comparator — major.minor.patch parsing, pre-release tags, range matching (^, ~, >=)
- JSON path evaluator — dot/bracket notation, array indexing, wildcard matching
- Cron expression parser — minute/hour/day/month/weekday fields, ranges, steps, special strings (@daily, @weekly)
- Markdown inline parser — bold, italic, code, links, escaping — many interacting state transitions
- IPv4/IPv6 address classifier — format detection, validation, mapped addresses, CIDR notation
- HTTP content negotiation — Accept header parsing, q-values, media type matching, wildcard handling
- Color format converter — hex/rgb/hsl/named, with clamping, alpha channel, shorthand expansion

**Criteria for good examples:**
1. Recognizable real-world utility (not contrived)
2. Rich branching (10+ distinct paths) without being artificially complex
3. Pure functions with no external dependencies
4. Interesting edge cases that reward thorough exploration
5. Implementable in both TS and Go with comparable branch structure

**Scope:** Pick 3-4 from the combined list. Implement in both examples/typescript/src/ and examples/go/. Add known-answer comments documenting expected branches and triggering inputs per CLAUDE.md conventions.

This pushes the branch count to 15+ distinct paths and exercises more interesting constraint-solving scenarios (numeric comparison, string lexical ordering, array-length comparisons for pre-release precedence). The parse-only version is too simple to be compelling.

description: Two mechanisms to auto-disable triage when it is not useful: (1) Skip-rate check: after 20 verdicts, if fewer than 10% result in Skip, disable triage — it is not producing value. Covers functions with mostly Unknown constraints or highly productive exploration. (2) Misprediction check: every 20th Skip verdict, execute anyway (sampling) and compare predicted path hash to actual. If misprediction rate exceeds 25% of samples, the constraint model is unreliable — disable triage. record_sample() tracks this. Constants: TRIAGE_SAMPLE_INTERVAL=20, MIN_VERDICTS_FOR_EVAL=20, MIN_SKIP_RATE=0.10, MAX_MISPREDICTION_RATE=0.25.
title: Adaptive triage self-disabling (skip-rate and misprediction)

description:
The orchestrator uses a single solver_timeout_ms for all Z3 queries. Simple constraints (integer comparison) solve in microseconds; complex ones (string operations, nested quantifiers) may need the full timeout or be intractable. A uniform timeout either wastes time on doomed queries or starves solvable-but-slow ones.

## Approach

Classify constraints by estimated complexity before submitting to Z3:

- **Trivial** (integer comparison, boolean): 50ms timeout
- **Moderate** (string equality, arithmetic composition): 500ms timeout
- **Complex** (string contains/regex, nested boolean trees): full configured timeout
- **Intractable heuristic** (constraints involving opaque function calls, crypto): skip or minimal attempt

Learn from observed solve times: if constraints of a given shape consistently timeout, deprioritize similar future queries. A lightweight online classifier (rolling mean solve time per constraint category) is sufficient — no ML needed.

## Budget model

Total solver time per function is bounded. Instead of N queries × fixed_timeout, allocate a total solver budget (e.g., 10s) and let the scheduler distribute it across queries weighted by estimated tractability. When budget is exhausted, remaining unsolved constraints are marked as deferred for potential later attempts (e.g., by the hybrid fuzzer from str-e0uy).

## Why deferred

Requires instrumentation of Z3 solve times (not currently tracked) and a constraint classification taxonomy. The current uniform timeout works acceptably for the constraint complexity seen in practice. This becomes important when exploring codebases with mixed simple/complex constraint profiles at scale.
title: Constraint complexity-aware solver timeout allocation

description:
**This is a future exploration item, not a gap in current functionality.** The current engine already handles arrays through its general concolic loop — it executes concretely, collects per-element branch decisions, negates them with Z3, and falls back to fuzzing for unsolvable constraints. Execution budgets and plateau detection prevent runaway exploration. This works.

The question is whether we can do *better* with array-aware strategies. Sequential processing of collection elements causes exponential path explosion (2^n paths for n elements with one branch each), and the engine currently has no special knowledge that it's exploring array contents vs any other parameter. A smarter approach could focus exploration budget more effectively.

Areas to investigate:

1. **Bounded unrolling** — Recognize array parameters and deliberately generate inputs at lengths 0, 1, 2, 3 rather than random 0-5. Most real bugs appear at small sizes. Spend solving budget on per-element branches at those sizes.

2. **Early-exit path detection** — Identify find/some/indexOf patterns where interesting paths are linear (found at index k, not found) rather than exponential.

3. **Quantified constraints** — Evaluate whether Z3 forall/exists quantifiers are practical for simple array predicates like 'exists element > 0'.

4. **Loop summarization** — Research feasibility of inferring loop invariants for common patterns (filter, map, reduce with thresholds).

5. **Coverage-directed array mutation** — AFL-style splice/insert/delete mutations for array contents, complementing the existing fuzzing fallback.

Prerequisite: overlay_solved_values would need to reconstruct nested JSON from solved flat values for some of these approaches.
title: Explore bounded symbolic execution for array/collection parameters

description:
Refactor the exploration pipeline into independent, composable stages with typed interfaces and CLI subcommands.

## Design Decisions (resolved)

1. **Concolic loop**: Separate Observe and Solve. Observe executes from all input sources (seeds, fuzz, user-provided, Z3-solved). Solve/Generate produces new candidates from analysis state. This creates a clean seam for the future pluggable generator trait (str-4bx).

2. **Canonical IR**: Rename ExplorationResult → ObservationOutput as the single pipeline type. Delete the adapter layer and From conversions. Add Serialize/Deserialize for caching. Transition via type alias to limit blast radius.

3. **CLI exposure**: Three new subcommands — `shatter observe`, `shatter analyze`, `shatter specify`. Solve stays internal. Each reads/writes stage output as JSON for caching and composability.

4. **scan_orchestrator**: Updated only as a consequence of IR type changes, not a separate goal.

## Stages

- **Observe** (CLI: `shatter observe`): Run function with many inputs (random, boundary, user-provided, Z3-solved), record all execution data. No analysis or spec generation. Output: ObservationOutput (JSON-serializable).
- **Analyze** (CLI: `shatter analyze`): Takes ObservationOutput, produces AnalyzeOutput (equivalence classes, behavior map, coverage metrics). Pure function, no I/O.
- **Solve** (internal only): Takes AnalyzeOutput + uncovered branch constraints, produces new candidate inputs. Will become the pluggable generator trait in str-4bx.
- **Specify** (CLI: `shatter specify`): Takes ObservationOutput + AnalyzeOutput, produces FunctionSpec. Pure function.

## Scope

This is an architectural refactor — the pieces exist but are coupled. Key changes:
- Rename ExplorationResult → ObservationOutput, unify the two result types
- Deduplicate analyze() and analyze_observation() into one function on ObservationOutput
- Make build_spec() take pipeline types instead of ExplorationResult directly
- Restructure orchestrator::explore() to separate observation from solving
- Add Serialize/Deserialize to stage output types for caching
- Add three CLI subcommands that read/write stage JSON
- Update scan_orchestrator to use new types

## Not in scope
- Pluggable generator trait (str-4bx)
- Concurrent observers/generators (str-frc)
- Protocol changes (BatchExecute etc.)
title: Staged pipeline: observe → analyze → solve → specify

description:
## Bug

The 'Symbolic expr' line in coverage metrics reports inflated constraint counts that vary between runs. For `categorizeUser` (5 branches), it shows values like 189, 194, 196, or 203 — and the number changes every run.

## Root Cause

`pipeline.rs:45-54` collects constraints from **every execution's branch path** without deduplication:

```rust
let all_constraints: Vec<SymConstraint> = observe
    .raw_results
    .iter()
    .flat_map(|(_, result)| {
        result.branch_path.iter().map(|d| d.constraint.clone())
    })
    .collect();
```

With 100 iterations, each execution contributes its branch path's constraints. An execution hitting 2 branches adds 2 entries; one hitting 4 branches adds 4. This produces ~200 total constraint observations for a function with only 5 unique branch conditions.

## Why it varies

Different random inputs take different paths. Some runs may have more executions hitting longer paths (3-4 branches) vs shorter ones (1 branch for the throw path), so the total observation count is nondeterministic.

## Expected behavior

The metric should report the number of **unique branch constraints** that are SymExpr vs Unknown — i.e., how many of the function's branch points have solvable symbolic expressions. For `categorizeUser` this should be ~5, not ~200.

## Fix

Deduplicate constraints by branch_id before counting. Each branch_id should contribute exactly one constraint classification (Expr or Unknown) regardless of how many times it was observed across executions.
title: Symbolic constraint count is inflated — counts per-execution observations, not unique constraints

description:
Add the contracts crate (design-by-contract) to shatter-core with a HIGH bar for usage — only where Rust's type system genuinely cannot express the invariant.

## Where contracts are warranted

### Solver bridge (primary target)
Crosses Rust/Z3 FFI boundary where Rust types can't help — Z3 sorts are runtime values.

- solve_for_new_path(): Pre: all Param nodes reference valid param indices. Post: solved values have types matching ParamInfo.
- infer_sort(): Post: returned Sort matches the TypeInfo of the referenced parameter.
- overlay_solved_values(): Pre: solved_values keys are valid param indices. Post: output length equals input length. Post: each output value's JSON type matches ParamInfo's TypeInfo.
- to_z3_expr(): Pre: SymExpr tree is well-formed (no dangling Param references).

### Protocol deserialization boundary
JSON from frontend subprocesses — types can't guarantee structural validity:

- parse_execute_result(): Post: branch_ids are non-empty, param_values length matches arity.
- parse_analyze_result(): Post: function signatures have valid parameter counts.

### Orchestrator state transitions

- add_to_worklist(): Post: worklist is non-empty after add.
- mark_path_covered(): Post: covered_paths contains the new hash.

### Where contracts are NOT warranted

- Functions where the type signature already encodes the contract
- Simple transformations where proptest is more valuable
- Internal helpers with a single call site
- Any function where the pre/postcondition restates the type signature

## Relationship with Kani

Complementary: contracts document intent inline and catch violations at runtime (debug builds). Kani proves contracts hold for ALL inputs statically. Write the contract first, optionally add Kani harness later for the most critical ones.

## Documentation

Add section to shatter-core/CLAUDE.md explaining the contracts usage policy with the high bar. Include examples of good contracts (solver bridge) vs bad contracts (restating types).
notes: Epic: str-08r7 (Formal methods and property-based verification)
title: Add contracts crate to shatter-core solver bridge and trust boundaries

description: Add CallEnter(call_site_id)/CallExit(call_site_id) scope events emitted by instrumented functions. Each instrumented function emits scope-enter at entry and scope-exit at return/throw. This handles: (1) Recursion — recursive calls produce nested CallEnter/CallExit, allowing path_hash to collapse repeated recursive frames the same way it collapses loop iterations. (2) Functional callbacks — map/filter/reduce/forEach invoke callbacks multiple times; if the callback is an instrumented function, its enter/exit markers let the core collapse repetitions. (3) Recursion containing loops containing recursion — scope markers nest arbitrarily and each scope level collapses independently. Implementation: inject scope-enter preamble and scope-exit at all return points (or use try/finally equivalent) in every instrumented function. No need to instrument call sites — only callees. Protocol: extend ScopeEvent to include CallEnter/CallExit variants.
title: Call scope markers for recursion and callback tracking

description:
When the concolic solver extracts path constraints (e.g. x > 10 && str.contains(s, "@")), we can evaluate those constraints against candidate inputs statically to predict which branch path they would take. If the predicted path is already in seen_paths, discard the input without executing it. This avoids the cost of spawning frontend execution for inputs that are overwhelmingly likely to be redundant.

Implementation sketch:
1. After each execution round, collect the SymConstraint entries from branch_path into a constraint index keyed by branch_id
2. For each candidate input, evaluate known constraints to predict the branch decisions
3. If all predicted branches map to an already-seen path hash, skip execution (fast path)
4. Periodically execute 'predicted-uninteresting' inputs anyway to catch constraint model errors (e.g. 1-in-20 sampling)

Constraints come from BranchDecision.constraint: SymConstraint in explorer.rs. Currently only used for Z3 solving, not for pre-filtering.

Limitations:
- Only works for branches with captured SymConstraints (Unknown constraints can't be evaluated)
- Cannot predict side effects, exceptions, or loop iteration counts
- Need a fallback sampling rate to catch model drift

Even a 50% fast-rejection rate would effectively double throughput of long farming runs.
design: Tristate verdict (Skip/Execute/Indeterminate). Branch trace templates from observed executions. Concrete SymExpr evaluator predicts branch outcomes without frontend IPC. Adaptive self-disabling via skip-rate and misprediction-rate checks. New module: shatter-core/src/triage.rs. Integration point: orchestrator explore() loop before frontend.send(Execute).
notes: Design plan: docs/plans/str-xmtw-symbolic-triage.md
title: Symbolic triage: use accumulated constraints to reject uninteresting inputs without execution

description:
## Bug

Running Go examples fails with:
```
[error] Exploration error for ClassifyNumber: frontend error: failed to deserialize response: missing field `constraint` at line 1 column 1785
```

## Root Cause

Go's `BranchDecision` struct in `protocol/types.go:170` has:
```go
Constraint *SymConstraint `json:"constraint,omitempty"`
```

When no symbolic constraint exists (the common case — Go doesn't do symbolic analysis), `Constraint` is `nil`, and `omitempty` causes Go to omit the field from JSON entirely.

Rust's `BranchDecision` in `execution_record.rs:36` has:
```rust
pub constraint: SymConstraint,  // required, no Option, no serde(default)
```

So Rust's serde rejects the response as missing a required field.

## Fix

Two-sided fix for robustness:

1. **Go (sender)**: In `convertBranchPath()` (`handler.go:320-338`), when `ConstraintJSON` is empty, emit an `Unknown` constraint with a descriptive hint instead of `nil`. Remove `omitempty` from the `Constraint` field tag.

2. **Rust (receiver)**: Add `#[serde(default)]` to the `constraint` field in `BranchDecision`, with a `Default` impl on `SymConstraint` that returns `Unknown { hint: String::new() }`. This makes the protocol resilient to any frontend that omits the field.

Both changes together ensure the protocol is robust in both directions.
title: Go frontend omits constraint field in BranchDecision, causing Rust deserialization failure

description:
Barrel files (index.ts with only 'export * from ./x' lines) are discovered as source files but yield zero functions. The scanner doesn't follow re-exports to find actual definitions. Affects remeda (150+ re-exports in index.ts) and any package using barrel patterns.

Design questions before implementation:
- How deep should re-export following go? (1 level? transitive?)
- How to handle circular re-exports?
- Should barrel files be excluded from discovery, or should re-exports be followed to source?
- How to handle external package re-exports (export * from 'lodash')?
- Performance impact of transitive resolution on large codebases?

Fix location: shatter-ts/src/analyzer.ts — needs import graph walking at analysis time. This is a feature addition, not a simple bug fix.
design:
## Current Architecture

analyzer.ts uses ts.createProgram([absolutePath], ...) — it creates a TS program with a single root file and a TypeChecker (line 37-49). Re-export statements have no function bodies, so they yield nothing.

The core sends one file at a time via the Analyze protocol command. batch_analyze.rs iterates over discovered files and sends individual analyze requests. At line 190, function_entry_from_analysis(file_path.clone(), func) hardcodes the analyzed file as the source for ALL returned functions.

## Critical Protocol Gap

FunctionAnalysis has no source_file field. If the frontend follows re-exports and returns functions from other files, the core will:
1. Associate them with the barrel file's path
2. Send instrument/execute commands targeting the barrel file
3. Fail because the barrel file has no function bodies

This means we MUST either:
A. Add an optional source_file field to FunctionAnalysis and have batch_analyze respect it, OR
B. Have the frontend NOT return functions from other files — instead, skip barrel files entirely and rely on the core also discovering the actual source files

## Recommended Approach: Option B (simpler)

Don't follow re-exports at analysis time. Instead:

1. In the file discovery phase (core side), detect barrel files and resolve their re-exports to actual source files
2. Add the resolved source files to the file list (deduplicating)
3. Skip analyzing the barrel file itself (or let it return empty, which is fine)

This avoids protocol changes entirely. The core already discovers files; it just needs to also discover files referenced by re-exports.

**Problem**: the core doesn't understand TS module resolution. It discovers files by glob pattern (*.ts), not by following imports.

## Revised Recommended Approach: Option A (frontend reports source_file)

### Protocol Change
Add to FunctionAnalysis in protocol.rs:
```rust
/// Source file where this function is actually defined.
/// When present, overrides the file from the analyze request.
/// Used when re-exports are followed.
#[serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")]
pub source_file: Option<String>,
```

Add matching field to TS protocol.ts FunctionAnalysis interface.

### batch_analyze.rs Change
At line 190, use func.source_file as the file_path if present:
```rust
let actual_path = func.source_file.as_ref()
    .map(|p| PathBuf::from(p))
    .unwrap_or_else(|| file_path.clone());
entries.push(function_entry_from_analysis(actual_path, func));
```

Also update the qualified name construction at line 187 to use the actual path.

### analyzer.ts Change
After the normal forEachChild walk, if results is empty:
1. Check if file has ExportDeclaration nodes with moduleSpecifier
2. Use checker.getSymbolAtLocation(sourceFile) to get module symbol
3. Call checker.getExportsOfModule(moduleSymbol) to get all exported symbols
4. For each symbol, resolve to declaration via getDeclarations()
5. If declaration is FunctionDeclaration or ArrowFunction in another file, analyze it
6. Set source_file field to the actual declaration's file path

### Deduplication
When the core discovers both barrel.ts and actual-source.ts, functions may be analyzed twice. batch_analyze should deduplicate by qualified name (using actual source_file path).

### Scope & Constraints
- Only follow re-exports from files that yield zero functions from direct scanning
- Skip external package re-exports (declarations in node_modules)
- TypeChecker.getExportsOfModule() handles transitive/circular re-exports internally
- Re-export following must also handle named re-exports: export { foo } from './bar'

### Test Plan
- Protocol: round-trip test for FunctionAnalysis with source_file
- Frontend: fixture barrel.ts that re-exports from helper-a.ts and helper-b.ts
- Integration: verify functions from re-exports are instrumented/executed against correct file
- Dedup: verify same function isn't explored twice if discovered via barrel AND direct file scan
title: TS frontend: function scanner doesn't follow barrel re-exports

description: The genetic_fitness module scores executions on 4 axes (coverage, proximity, unknown_bonus, novelty) but none capture how deep into nested control flow an input penetrates. An input reaching branch depth 15 is strictly more valuable than one reaching depth 2 — it's closer to exploring the deepest, hardest-to-reach behaviors. Add a 5th 'depth' component: normalize branch_path.len() via asymptotic formula (len / (len + k)) and add it to FitnessWeights/FitnessBreakdown. This is a prerequisite for wiring fitness into the orchestrator.
title: Add execution depth axis to genetic fitness function

description:
Build parameterized constraint templates from observed concrete traces, then symbolically unroll K times and use Z3 to find inputs (including iteration counts) that reach specific branches. Most powerful but most invasive technique.

Key components:
- symbolic_unroll.rs (NEW): IterationTemplate, StateUpdate, UnrolledFormula types
- Hybrid approach: concrete execution → template extraction (observe 2+ iterations) → generalize (replace concrete values with symbolic __k) → unroll to depth K
- Triggers as fallback when standard solving stalls on loop branches
- Protocol: LoopBodyState for per-iteration symbolic state snapshots
- Frontend: enhance buildDataFlowMap to track variable mutations inside loops (significant scope increase)
- Cap unroll depth based on solver timeout

HIGH EFFORT — 10-12 files, most invasive frontend change. Depends on techniques 5 (InductionVar) + 6 (ITE expressions).
notes: See docs/plans/loop-analysis-compiler-techniques.md — Technique 7. Deferred. Depends on techniques 5 and 6.
title: Loop analysis: bounded symbolic unrolling

description:
Implement test impact analysis (TIA) to accelerate local development by running only the tests affected by changed files. The system maintains a coverage map recording which tests touch which source files, then uses that map to select the minimal test set for a given set of changes.

## Design

**Coverage map** stored at `.shatter/test-markers/coverage-map.yaml`:
- Forward map: test → {file: blob_hash} (update direction)
- Reverse map: file → [tests] (query direction, derived from forward)
- Blob hashes via `git hash-object` for content-addressable comparison

**Query flow**: `git diff --name-only` → changed files → reverse lookup → union of affected tests → format runner-specific filter

**Update flow**: after test run with coverage instrumentation, parse coverage output → update forward map entries for tests that ran → rebuild reverse map. Atomic write via temp file + rename.

**Staleness tracking**: each file entry records the blob hash at recording time. Changed hash = stale = must re-run associated tests. Import-change detection: if a file's imports changed (not just function bodies), broadly invalidate its dependents.

**Cold start**: empty map treats all files as unmapped → falls back to crate/package-scoped testing. Map populates incrementally as tests run with coverage.

**Tiered test markers**: tree hash (from `git write-tree`) stored at `.shatter/test-markers/<tier>` after a tier's tests pass. Pre-commit hook compares current tree hash against the required tier's marker.

## Flags

- `shatter test` (or wrapper script) — run only affected tests based on coverage map
- `shatter test --all` — run all tests regardless, bypassing TIA selection (always available as escape hatch)
- `shatter test --record` — force coverage recording to refresh the map
- `shatter test --tier=<quick|standard|full|e2e>` — run the specified tier and write its marker on success

## Runner integration

- Rust: `cargo-nextest` with `-E 'test(=name)'` filter expressions (requires installing cargo-nextest). Falls back to `cargo test -- name1 name2` for module-level filtering.
- TypeScript: Vitest with explicit file paths or `--testNamePattern`
- Go: `go test ./package/ -run 'TestA|TestB'`

## Coverage collection

LLVM source-based coverage via `cargo-llvm-cov --json` for Rust (~10-20% overhead). Vitest `--coverage` for TS. `go test -coverprofile` for Go. For local dev loops where tests take 5-15s, always-on coverage is acceptable (~1-3s overhead). Alternatively, periodic recording with `--record` flag.

## Current test landscape (for reference)

- 1,487 Rust tests across ~60 modules + 3 integration test files
- 195 Go tests across protocol/instrument/generators packages
- 6 TS test files
- No cargo-nextest installed yet
- No cross-cutting test groups; filtering is by module/crate/package name
- Existing tiers: Quick (cargo test), Standard (+clippy), Full (+TS+Go), E2E (+e2e_concolic), Walkthrough (+demo script)
notes: Plan saved to docs/plans/str-zwgc-test-impact-analysis.md. Exploration confirmed reuse of scm.rs (GitProvider, run_git), file_lock.rs (FileLock), existing serde_yaml dep. Three phases: P1 core data model + CLI, P2 coverage recording, P3 pre-commit integration.
title: Test impact analysis: coverage-based selective test execution
